Citation: ICES Working Group on Widely Distributed Stocks. ICES advice 2023, Table 10.
Description: Results of annual analytical stock assessment released by ICES in October.
Motivation: This component refers to the enter of new individuals (recruits; number of herring at age 2) to the future herring biomass. It can be used to predict year-class strength. This component is considered the ecosystem overview, and it can contribute to IEA based on interactions with other specie (predation), understanding of fisheries management.
ConceptualUnderstanding: This indicator is the model output from the number of age 2 herring, and it is measured the recruitment to the stock level. It is directly linked to the indicandum, i.e. the number of herring at age 2 in the ocean (total recruitment of herring).
ScientificConsensus: This indicator and its interpretation have a well-established consensus. It is widely used in fisheries science and management.  Recruitment is not a part of GOOS EOV.
QuantitativeDimension: The indicator is measured quantitatively derived from analytical stock assessment models in the WGWIDE ICES working group.
Precision: The indicator is reported with uncertainty estimates (95% Confidence Interval), labelled in the WGINOR as HerringRecrutimentLow and HerringRecrutimentHigh.
Bias: The level of bias can be estimated annually and report at the WGWIDE report. Annual updates affect the time-series.
Updating: The indicator is updated annually through ICES WGWIDE stock assessments. The indicator is a model output and indicator values for the entire time series are updated every year.  The lag between data collection and reporting varies but is less than 1 year.
SpaceTimeCoverage: Coverage area is the Norwegian Sea ecosystem and adjacent areas and cover annually.
LevelOfIntegration: The primary focus of the indicator is to the juvenile herring stock.
Cost: It requires time and capacity for the update every year from the annual WGWIDE-report. Therefore, the resources needed from WGINOR are minor. The stock assessments are funded by the individual institutes participating.
History: Time-series is available since 1988 and updated annually. There is an existing time series for it.
Simplicity: The modelling process required to derive the indicator is complex. It requires moderately heavy computation (few hours on a laptop). It requires strong expertise in stock assessment (age-based analytical model [SAM]).
Transparency: The process for computing the indicator is well described. The indicator is part of the TAF ICES’ Transparent Assessment Framework), also is under the FAIR (Findable, Accessible, Interoperable, Reusable) scope.
DataType: Model-derived from stock assessments. Data are publicly available from ICES WGWIDE
Sensitivity: There is a liner response, increase in the number of age 2 increase the juvenile herring stock.
Specificity: Yes, changes in the indicator value are dependent on egg production, mortality. Specifically tied to the size of incoming year classes. While affected by multiple factors, its interpretation is well established and central to stock dynamics.
LagAndLeadTime: At the annual time scale, the indicator responds in synchrony with the indicandum.
ManagementRelevance: Yes, the indicator is reported by ICES (International Council for the exploration of the Sea) and used for the management of the species. The indicator is reported internationally.
ManagementUse: The indicator supports decision support
ReferencePoint: The direction of change is interpretable.
PriorKnowledge: The indicator is well known by broad audience including scientists, non-marine scientists, to managers, to stakeholders, and to the public.
Clarity: It is easy to understand the meaning of the indicator and interpret correctly by other non-scientists.
Reaction: Difficult to make assumptions about reaction

Citation: ICES Working Group on Widely Distributed Stocks. ICES advice 2023, Table 10.
Description: Results of annual analytical stock assessment released by ICES in October.
Motivation: The indicandum is the total number of 1 year old blue whiting. The indicator is the back-calculated number of 1 year old blue whiting from stock assessment model and is used as proxy for recruitment to the adult stock. This indicator is described in the ecosystem status summary and ecosystem overview.
ConceptualUnderstanding: The blue whiting recruitment indicator (number at age 1) is conceptually tightly linked to the indicandum (number of blue whiting at age 1).
ScientificConsensus: There is strong scientific consensus on using back-calculated number at age x as proxy for recruitment strength. Recruitment is being widely used in stock assessment and fisheries management. Recruitment is not a part of GOOS EOV.
QuantitativeDimension: Recruitment is measured quantitatively as output from stock assessment models.
Precision: The blue whiting recruitment is given with 95% confidence limits. Signal-to-noise ratio is generally high.  Time-series with lower and higher confidence limits are given together with the recruitment time-series (BlueWhitingRecruitmentLow and BlueWhitingRecruitmentHigh).
Bias: The level of interannual bias in recruitment is reported in annual advice on fishing opportunities. Also, the stock-assessment report provides figures describing bias as perceived by conducting peel-back calculations and ‘Mohn’s Rho’. The level of bias can be significant – most often in the latest years of the time series.
Updating: Recruitment is updated annually. The indicator is a model output and indicator values for the entire time series are updated every year. The delay between observation/measurement and reporting is 1 year. Standard sentence for all model output data: “The indicator is a model output and indicator values for the entire time series are updated every year."
SpaceTimeCoverage: The spatial extent of blue whiting recruitment is for the distribution area of blue whiting i.e. Norwegian Sea of relevance for this eco-region. Temporally, the extent is back to 1981 with one value per year.
LevelOfIntegration: The primary focus of blue whiting recruitment is on species level and does not integrate across different components or sectors.
Cost: Blue whiting recruitment is obtained from the WGWIDE working group, and there is limited expertise required in WGINOR to prepare the indicator. National institutes fund data collections and model work. The ongoing work is relatively secure in the long term.
History: The indicator goes back to 1981. The methodology has advanced over time, but the entire time-series is recalculated at each annual update.
Simplicity: The model approach used to produce the recruitment indicator is advanced and computationally heavy. This work requires expert-skills.
Transparency: The process for computing blue whiting recruitment indicator is described in WGWIDE-reports and underlying Stock Annex. The whole process is available in TAF and is FAIR.
DataType: The data type of blue whiting recruitment is model-output. ICES. 2025. Blue whiting (Micromesistius poutassou) in subareas 1-9, 12, and 14 (Northeast Atlantic and adjacent waters. In Report of the ICES Advisory Committee, 2025. ICES Advice 2025, whb.27.1-91214. https://doi.org/10.17895/ices.advice.27202938
Sensitivity: Variations in the recruitment indicator are linearly related to change in the indicandum.
Specificity: Changes in blue whiting recruitment can be specifically linked to incoming year-classes. It can be affected by multiple factors (e.g. varying mortality, size of spawning area), but its interpretation is well established and central to stock dynamics.
LagAndLeadTime: The recruitment indicator responds to the indicandum at the annual time scale. There is some potential for forecasting blue whiting recruitment some years ahead based on climatic conditions such as wind stress curl.
ManagementRelevance: Yes, the indicator is reported by ICES (International Council for the exploration of the Sea) and used for the management of the species. The indicator is reported internationally.
ManagementUse: The recruitment indicator is used in forecasting the stock assessment two years ahead and is therefore used as support for management decisions.
ReferencePoint: The direction of change in blue whiting recruitment is clearly interpretable. This indicator does not have calculated reference points.
PriorKnowledge: The indicator is well known by broad audience including scientists, non-marine scientists, to managers, to stakeholders, and to the public.
Clarity: It is easy to understand the meaning of the indicator and interpret it correctly by other non-scientists.
Reaction: It is difficult to make assumptions about reactions about the indicator.

Citation: ICES Working Group on Widely Distributed Stocks. ICES advice 2023, Table 10.
Description: Results of annual analytical stock assessment released by ICES in October.
Motivation: This indicator estimates the number of 2-year-old mackerel entering the stock annually. Recruitment at age 2 is a key driver of future stock size and productivity. It can be used to predict year-class strength. This component is considered in the ecosystem overview, and it can contribute to IEA based on interactions with other specie (predation), understanding of fisheries management.
ConceptualUnderstanding: This indicator is the model output from the number of age 2 mackerel, and it is measured the recruitment to the stock level. It is directly linked to the indicandum the total number of age 2 mackerel (recruitment of mackerel).
ScientificConsensus: This indicator and its interpretation have a well-established consensus. It is widely used in fisheries science and management.  Recruitment is not a part of GOOS EOV.
QuantitativeDimension: The indicator is measured quantitatively derived from analytical stock assessment models in the WGWIDE ICES working group.
Precision: The indicator is reported with uncertainty estimates (95% Confidence Interval), labelled in the WGINOR as MackerelRecrutimentLow and MackerelRecrutimentHigh.
Bias: The level of bias can be estimated annually and report at the WGWIDE report. Annual updates affect the time-series.
Updating: The indicator is updated annually through ICES WGWIDE stock assessments. The indicator is a model output and indicator values for the entire time series are updated every year. The lag between data collection and reporting varies but is less than 1 year.
SpaceTimeCoverage: Covers the full spatial extent of the Northeast Atlantic mackerel stock (Norwegian Sea ecosystem and adjacent areas) and is annual.
LevelOfIntegration: The primary focus of the indicator is to the juvenile mackerel stock.
Cost: It requires time and capacity for the update every year from the annual WGWIDE-report. Therefore, the resources needed from WGINOR are minor. The stock assessments are funded by the individual institutes participating.
History: Time-series is available since 1998 and updated annually. There is an existing time series for it.
Simplicity: Complex and heavy computation is necessary. Also, significant effort on research cruises and biological samples from commercial landings. High degree of expertise is needed to perform the stock assessment.
Transparency: The process for computing the indicator is well described. The indicator is part of the TAF ICES’ Transparent Assessment Framework), also is under the FAIR (Findable, Accessible, Interoperable, Reusable) scope.
DataType: Model-derived from stock assessments. Data are publicly available from ICES WGWIDE
Sensitivity: There is a liner response, it is highly sensitive to changes in early life survival. Reflects cumulative effects of spawning conditions, larval drift, food supply, and mortality.
Specificity: Specifically tied to the size of incoming year classes. While affected by multiple factors, its interpretation is well established and central to stock dynamics.
LagAndLeadTime: At the annual time scale, the indicator responds in synchrony with the indicandum.
ManagementRelevance: Yes, the indicator is reported by ICES (International Council for the exploration of the Sea) and used for the management of the species. The indicator is reported internationally.
ManagementUse: The indicator supports decision support
ReferencePoint: The direction of change is interpretable.
PriorKnowledge: The indicator is well known by broad audience including scientists, non-marine scientists, to managers, to stakeholders, and to the public.
Clarity: It is easy to understand the meaning of the indicator and interpret correctly by other non-scientists.
Reaction: Difficult to make assumptions about reaction

Citation: ICES Working Group on Widely Distributed Stocks. ICES advice 2023, Table 10.
Description: Results of annual analytical stock assessment released by ICES in October.
Motivation: The indicandum is the biomass of Norwegian spring spawning herring that has matured sexually to have the capability to spawn; the generally used term for this indicator is Spawning Stock Biomass (SSB). SSB gives good indications on the status of the NSS herring stock and as such is considered relevant to include in the ecosystem status summary and the ecosystem overview. SSB of a major fish stock is also considered as valuable input to several analyses – both conducted and planned – in integrated ecosystem assessment (IEA).
ConceptualUnderstanding: SSB is a measure of the adult NSS herring stock. Conceptually, it increases as incoming year classes recruit to the adult stock and individual fish grow in weight, and it decreases as fish are removed from the stock, either naturally from being predated – or less frequently self-dying – or from being fished out of the stock.
ScientificConsensus: The scientific consensus on SSB is strong. It is widely used in fisheries science and management.
QuantitativeDimension: SSB is measured quantitatively derived from analytical stock assessment models in the WGWIDE ICES working group.
Precision: The level of precision (95% confidence intervals) is standard output of the current stock assessment. The higher and lower confidence limits are also in the WGINOR indicator list.  Annual updates of the SSB time series affect the entire time-series – and the latest years with uncertain recruitment estimates can change significantly from one year to another.
Bias: Level of bias (measured as ‘retrospective pattern’ among years) is estimated annually. The last approximately five years the level of bias has been considered low. Annual updates affect the time-series.
Updating: SSB is updated annually. The delay from observation to estimate is zero to one year. Catch data are updated the year before and survey data are mostly updated in the assessmentyear.
SpaceTimeCoverage: The temporal extent of the indicator is one year, and the spatial coverage is Norwegian Sea ecosystem scale.
LevelOfIntegration: The level of integration is the adult NSS herring stock.
Cost: SSB of NSS herring is output from the annual WGWIDE-report. Therefore, the resources needed from WGINOR are minor. The stock assessments are funded by the individual participating institutes .
History: NSS herring SSB has been measured since 1907. The methodology has changed over time and the current time series being updated annually goes back to 1988. The uncertainty limits are available back to 1988.
Simplicity: Complex and heavy computation is necessary. Also, significant effort on research cruises and biological samples from commercial landings. High degree of expertise is needed to perform the stock assessment.
Transparency: The stock assessment and process are available in TAF (ICES’ Transparent Assessment Framework). Consequently, it is also FAIR (Findable, Accessible, Interoperable, Reusable).
DataType: Model-derived from stock assessments. Data are publicly available from ICES WGWIDE (adviceXplorer )
Sensitivity: There is a linear response. Variations in modelled SSB reflect the changes in the spawning stock biomass of NSS herring.
Specificity: Changes in modelled SSB can be attributed specifically to changes in the biomass.
LagAndLeadTime: SSB responds to changes in biomass in the assessment year. SSB is being used as basis for short term forecast of the fisheries.
ManagementRelevance: SSB of NSS herring is used in management. It is reported with the catch advice from ICES and is reported to management organisation (Coastal States) and to the public. It is being used as basis for the annual catch advice and sub-sequent quota-sharing to nations. It is also used in harvest control rules.
ManagementUse: SSB is primarily used for decision support.
ReferencePoint: Changes in the direction of SSB are clearly interpretable; increasing SSB means increasing stock. Limit, target and MSY reference points are estimated for SSB.
PriorKnowledge: SSB of NSS herring is widely known to a broad audience including scientists, managers, fishermen and the public.
Clarity: It is relatively easy to communicate changes and levels of the SSB-indicator.
Reaction: Since changes in SSB are clearly interpretable, it is assumed that reactions to information about SSB are proportional to the intended message.

Sensitivity: There is a linear response. Variations in modelled SSB reflect the changes in the spawning stock biomass of blue whiting.
Specificity: Changes in modelled SSB can be attributed specifically to changes in the biomass.
LagAndLeadTime: SSB responds to changes in biomass in the assessment year. SSB is being used as basis for short term forecast of the fisheries.
ManagementRelevance: The indicator is reported by ICES (International Council for the exploration of the Sea) and used for the management of the species. The indicator is reported internationally.
ManagementUse: The indicator is primarily used for decision support
ReferencePoint: The direction of change is interpretable; increasing SSB means increasing stock. Limit, target, and MSY reference points are estimated for SSB.
PriorKnowledge: The indicator is well known by broad audience of scientists but also non-scientists, managers, stakeholders and public.
Clarity: It is easy to understand the meaning of the indicator and interpret correctly by non-scientists.
Reaction: Since changes in SSB are clearly interpretable, it is assumed that reactions to information about SSB are proportional to the intended message.

Citation: ICES Working Group on Widely Distributed Stocks. ICES advice 2023, Table 10.
Description: Results of annual analytical stock assessment released by ICES in October.
Motivation: Commercial fishing is one of the main pressures on the Norwegian Sea ecosystem. F estimates the annual harvest rate of the Norwegian spring spawning herring stock. It is vital information for IEA as commercial fishing poses a considerable pressure on the Norwegian Sea ecosystem hence is reported as part of the Ecosystem Overview but is not reported in the Ecosystem Status Summary.
ConceptualUnderstanding: The Herring fishing mortality (F) is the instantaneous fishing mortality rate, applied continuously over time. It does not directly represent the annual proportion of the stock removed. For example, if F = 0.2, then e−0.2≈0.82, meaning about 18% of the stock is harvested annually. The indicandum (in situ fishing rate) is the estimated proportion of the herring stock removed annually by commercial fisheries or in other words the pressure asserted on the ecosystem by selective extraction of species. The relationship between the F value (indicator, estimated by model) and the harvest rate is stable over time.
ScientificConsensus: F is a key variable estimated in analytical stock assessment and frequently used as management tool for exploitation of fish stocks in Europe and North America. It is also used to evaluate human pressure on marine ecosystems in IEA. It is not a part of GOOS EOV.
QuantitativeDimension: The indicator is quantitative, derived from analytical stock assessment models in the WGWIDE ICES working group.
Precision: Uncertainty estimates are provided as 95% confidence intervals (in WGINOR data series, it is labelled HerringFLow and HerringFHigh).
Bias: F is estimated from a stock assessment model but not directly observed. Any bias in input data to the assessment model will cause bias in estimations of F. Bias is not quantitatively estimated. Qualitatively is can be assumed to be low and we can trust the estimated values.
Updating: The indicator is updated annually through ICES WGWIDE stock assessments. The indicator is a model output and indicator values for the entire time series are updated every year. The lag between data collection and reporting varies but is less than 1 year.
SpaceTimeCoverage: The indicator reflects fishing pressure on NSS herring in the Norwegian Sea ecosystem (and adjacent areas; Icelandic and Norwegian waters). The indicator is integrated over annual time periods (one value per year).
LevelOfIntegration: The indicator level is the blue whiting stock. It measures the pressure of “selective extraction of species”.
Cost: The indicator is produced by WGWIDE for annual stock assessment of mackerel. Sampling and calculation cost is covered by stock assessment funding, from many nations, which can be considered secure funding in the long term.
History: Fishing pressure for NSS-herring has a long time series (available from 1988). It is routinely updated.
Simplicity: The indicator is provided ready-to-use by another ICES working group. The index is complex to calculate and requires advanced knowledge on stock assessment methods.
Transparency: The stock assessment process is transparent (archived in the ICES Transparent Assessment Framework (TAF)) and documented in WGWIDE reports and Stock Annexes.  The indicator is also under the FAIR (Findable, Accessible, Interoperable, Reusable) scope.
DataType: The indicator is an output from stock assessment model. Model calculations are available in TAF, ensuring adherence to the FAIR principle.
Sensitivity: The relationship between the F value and the harvest rate is stable over time. It is an exponential decay relationship.
Specificity: Changes in fishing mortality generally reflect changes in fishing pressure, but they can also be influenced by model assumptions, data quality, and natural variability in the system, so they are not always unambiguous.
LagAndLeadTime: F reacts to changes in the harvest rate on an annual basis. It can be projected in stock assessment models under different harvest scenarios.
ManagementRelevance: F is reported by ICES WGWIDE at the request of NEAFC, presenting Coastal States participating in the NSS herring fishery. It is a management tool used to control the harvest rate on the blue whiting stock.
ManagementUse: The indicator supports diagnostic and decision-support roles. It informs management decisions, especially when F exceeds or is below reference points like FMSY or biomass triggers
ReferencePoint: Changes are clearly interpretable: higher F means increased fishing pressure. ICES defines reference points, such as FMSY which are typically normative and target-based (management goals).
PriorKnowledge: The indicator is well known among marine scientists worldwide, as well as to managers and stakeholders in regions with commercial fisheries. However, it is likely less familiar to the public.
Clarity: Stakeholders and managers in commercial fisheries are likely to understand the indicator clearly and correctly interpret the associated variability.  The uncertainty around F is manageable to explain to stakeholders and managers.
Reaction: Managers and stakeholders in commercial fisheries are expected to respond appropriately. The public, however, is unlikely to understand or react to the indicator.
