Citation: ICES Working Group on Widely Distributed Stocks. ICES advice 2023, Table 10.
Description: Input for annual analytical stock assessment released by ICES in October. Sum of official national records of commercial catchs as complied by ICES WGWIDE annually.
Motivation: This indicator represents the biomass that is removed through fishing. It contributes to our understanding of how fishing may affect the blue whiting stock. Catch forms part of the input data for stock assessment but the trend is not reported in the ecosystem status summary or the ecosystem overview. Catch is an important component for IEA because it contributes to selective extraction of species through fishing. At the same time, it can be an indicator of population state and is related to socio-economics.
ConceptualUnderstanding: The indicator (catch in biomass) corresponds to the indicandum, catch, describing it in biomass units. Catch is a measure of stock exploitation through fishing based on standardized data reporting from the nations participating in blue whiting fisheries. The relationship between the indicator and the indicandum can be stable over time given the catch is reported accurately.
ScientificConsensus: Catch forms part of input data for annual analytical stock assessment. The values represent the sum of official national records of commercial catches as complied by ICES WGWIDE annually. Catch is not recommended as an indicator by international institutions such as GOOS at present.
Precision: The indicator does not have uncertainty estimates.
Bias: The data are compiled from national logbooks and landing statistics. Landings are usually considered relatively well reported. There are no quantitative estimates for bias.
Updating: The indicator is updated annually.
SpaceTimeCoverage: The spatial extent of this indicator is the Northeast Atlantic. The indicator is integrated over annual time periods (one value per year).
LevelOfIntegration: The level of integration is vessel, fleet and fishery level of the stock. It indirectly integrates to the other ecosystem components as fishing is considered a pressure and can influence food-web and ecosystem dynamics.
Cost: This indicator is produced within WGWIDE therefore the resourced needed from WGINOR are minor. It requires time and capacity to update every year. The financial support for the data collection is covered by stock assessment funding and is likely to continue long term.
History: The time series ranges from 1981-present and has been reported in a consistent manner.
Simplicity: The indicator compilation process does not require heavy computation.
Transparency: The time series of total catches is on TAF therefore follows the FAIR principles.
DataType: The data contains in situ observations. They are available through the ICES WGWIDE report and on TAF.
Sensitivity: The catch is not linearly related to fishing pressure as the stock is managed using a long-term management strategy based on analytical stock assessment with a specific F and biological reference points. Catch may be a bias indicator of stock status because of overfishing due to lack of international sharing agreements between fishing parties.
Specificity: Changes in fishing pressure have direct impact on catch volume. Estimated stock size can also have impact on catches. Additionally, environmental conditions can affect fish distribution. Therefore, the interpretation is not simple.
LagAndLeadTime: At the annual time scale, the indicator responds in synchrony with the indicandum which is stock size. The indicator can be forecasted in the short term for up to two years. Catches can forecast stock size in the short term.
ManagementRelevance: Catches are regularly reported through ICES WGWIDE and are part of the advice process. Catch data are requested and used by ICES to provide scientific advice. The indicator is reported internationally within the ICES advisory system, which supports coastal state negotiations.
ManagementUse: These data form the basis for TAC recommendations, which are central to blue whiting fisheries management.
ReferencePoint: There are no reference points for this indicator. Catch is dependent on stock size via TAC. Catch, however, has exceeded TAC in recent years. Directionality is unclear without reference to F and SSB.
PriorKnowledge: Catch is a common indicator and is known by a broad audience.
Clarity: Catch trends are generally understood by non-scientists (managers, stakeholders, and public).
Reaction: It is considered adequate and proportional as the indicator is well understood.

FullName: Herring average weight at age 6 in the stock
Description: Input for annual analytical stock assessment released by ICES in October. Data compiled by WGWIDE. The mean weight-at-age (age 6) in the stock was based on the acoustic survey (ICES code) in the wintering area until 2008. Since then, the mean weight-at-age in the stock was derived from samples taken in the fishery in the same area and at the same time as the wintering surveys were conducted in.
Motivation: The indicandum is weight of age-6 herring during the overwintering period, which is related to ecological circumstances such as feeding conditions and density dependent growth. Weight at age 6 is included in the ecosystem status summary.
ConceptualUnderstanding: Conceptually, weight at age is related to ecological circumstances such as feeding conditions and density dependent growth. For NSS herring, size at age has been related to ecological conditions in several peer-reviewed studies. The relationship between indicator and indicandum are stable over time.
ScientificConsensus: Size at age is a relatively common indicator for ecological conditions.
QuantitativeDimension: Weight at age is measured quantitatively and is based on commercial samples,before 2008 based on survey samples, however.
Precision: Level of precision can be estimated based on standard statistic methods (mean, variance etc.) earlier in the process than what is reported to this group. Level of precision is, however, not reported with the indicator.  Generally, sample sizes for age 6 herring are of such magnitude that noise is small relative to signal.
Bias: Level of bias can be expected to be low, as 6-year-old herring is fully recruited to the standardized sampling time and area. Estimate of bias is not reported with the indicator.
Updating: Weight at age is updated annually in connection with stock assessment. The delay from measurement to reporting is less than a year.
SpaceTimeCoverage: The indicator is based on standard area  Norwegian shelf areas and time overwintering but is expected to reflect conditions on stock level. Weight at age 6 is partly an accumulated response over lifetime until then – but can also be affected by annual changes in ecological conditions.
LevelOfIntegration: The primary focus of the indicator is adult herring as represented by 6-year-old herring.
Cost: Data collection and indicator preparation is conducted by IMR, Norway and is funded nationally. Weight at age is used as input to stock assessment and is expected to be continually updated. No extra expertise is needed in WGINOR to prepare the indicator.
History: Weight at age of NSS herring is available to WGINOR back to 1950. The most recent change in methodology was in 2008, when the basis for calculating weight at age changed from survey data (survey discontinued) to use commercial catches in the same area and time, as the survey used to be.
Simplicity: The process to produce weight-at-age is relatively straight-forward to calculate based on raw-data.
Transparency: The process for computing the indicator is described in stock assessment reports and stock annex (WGWIDE reports). The data the indicator is based on are not publicly available in TAF.
DataType: The indicator is calculated using in situ data which are biological samples from commercial catches and research surveys. The raw data used to calculate the index are not publicly available but can be requested. Contact person for the raw data is WGWIDE blue whiting data coordinator: Are Salthaug, IMR, Norway.
Sensitivity: Variations in the indicator reflect changes in the indicandum linearly.
Specificity: Changes in weight at age can be attributed specifically to changes in size of adult herring. Variations in weight-at-age are affected by several factors and cannot be interpreted unambiguously, since there are both accumulated responses (such as growth over time and competition) and more time limited responses (e.g. density dependent effects of year class strength based early in life).
LagAndLeadTime: The weight-at-age indicator responds to changes in the indicandum intraannually. There might be some potential to forecast the indicator a couple of years into the future – given that the density dependent effects are found to be sufficiently robust.
ManagementRelevance: The indicator is reported annually by ICES Working Group on Widely Distributed Stocks (WGWIDE) and is used in analytical stock assessment for NSS herring.
ManagementUse: The indicator itself is not used for decision support, but it is used as input to stock assessment, which gives basis for advice on fishing opportunities.
ReferencePoint: Directions of change are clearly interpretable. There is no reference point calculated for weight-at-age.
PriorKnowledge: Weight-at-age is widely used in marine science but may not be as widely known to a broader audience, such as the public.
Clarity: It is assumed that non-scientists can understand the meaning of the indicator, as it is intuitively easy to understand.
Reaction: It is assumed that reactions to information about weight-at-age will be proportional.

FullName: Blue whiting average weight at age 4 in the stock
Description: Input for annual analytical stock assessment released by ICES in October. Data compiled by WGWIDE. The weight-at-age  (age 4) for the stock is assumed the same as the weight-at-age for the catch. Weight-at-age for the total catch is calculated using catch-at-age data submitted annually to WGWIDE by national data providers. (Anna: get more details about this is calculated from Patricia).
Motivation: The indicator aims to estimate blue whiting average weight-at-age, at stock level, during spawning time. It reflects cumulative somatic growth of a year class from hatch to age 4, when the majority of individuals are mature. It contributes to IEA as it is a key variable for calculating stock biomass, in combination with numbers-at-age, and is also a key life history trait. The indictor is included in WGINOR Ecosystem Status Summary.
ConceptualUnderstanding: Weight-at-age is a key life history trait that reflects the somatic growth of a year class at a specific age. It provides insight into a fish's growth rate and how it allocates energy to growth over time. It impacts both survival and reproduction. It is also used to assess the health of a fish population density dependnet growht, and can reflect the effects of environmental changes, such as shifts in ambient temperature or prey availability. It also serves as an indicator of evolutionary pressures on a stock.
ScientificConsensus: The indicator is widely accepted in the scientific community for use in stock assessment and for researching stock biology and ecology. It is used in many marine ecosystems and is also recommended by GOOS EOV as a key measurement for fish, as it is vital for calculating total fish biomass.
Precision: The indicator is a point estimate which is not reported with uncertainty estimates.
Bias: The level of bias is not estimated. Potential bias may come from high variability in the number and location of samples collected for each stock component from year to year. The bias can be expected to be low. We can trust absolute values unless specifically reported issues with sampling.
Updating: Updated annually. Sampling during the spawning season; reporting typically within 6–9 months, in the ICES WGWIDE reports.
SpaceTimeCoverage: The indicator has the spatial scale of the stock’s spawning area. It is calculated annually and integrated over the spawning season.
LevelOfIntegration: Focused at the species and age class level. Reflects cumulative growth over four years of an age cohort.
Cost: The indicator is produced by WGWIDE for annual stock assessment of blue whiting. Sampling and calculation cost if coverage by stock assessment funding, from many nations, which can be considered secure funding.
History: The indicator has been estimated as an input series for stock assessment for decades. The current time series version begins in 1981.
Simplicity: The indicator is provided ready-to-use by another ICES working group. While the index is easy to compute, the challenging part is ensuring adequate spatial and temporal collection of samples.
Transparency: The calculations are described in ICES WGWIDE reports. The indicator values are available in TAF, but raw data used to calculate them are not.
DataType: The indicator is calculated using in situ data which are biological samples from commercial catches and research surveys. The raw data used to calculate the index are not publicly available but can be requested. Contact person for the raw data as of March 2026, is WGWIDE blue whiting data coordinator:Patricia Goncalves (patricia@ipma.pt).
Sensitivity: Weight-at-age measures somatic growth of year-classes as they enter the spawning stock. The indicator is linearly related to changes in somatic growth between year classes. Itis sensitive to changes in environmental conditions affecting growth (e.g. prey availability, temperature), and both intraspecific (within species) and interspecific (between species) competition for prey.
Specificity: Changes in weight-at-age is a response to changes in somatic growth of blue whiting year classes as they enter the spawning stock. Weight-at-age is linearly related to changes in somatic growth (assuming non-biased sampling).
LagAndLeadTime: The indicator response to changes in somatic growth between year-classes and on annual scale. The response is directly to the indicandum.
ManagementRelevance: The indicator is reported annually by ICES Working Group on Widely Distributed Stocks (WGWIDE) and is use in analytical stock assessment for blue whiting.
ManagementUse: The indicator is primarily used for diagnostic purposes. It is used as input data in the stock assessment model.
ReferencePoint: There are no reference points for the indicator.  The direction of change can be clearly interpreted. A higher weight-at-age 4 suggests that a year class experienced good somatic growth during its juvenile life history stage and that it is entering the spawning stock in better physiological state compared a year class with lower weight-at-age.
PriorKnowledge: The indicator is widely known to marine scientists and likely to most managers and stakeholders. It is difficult to assess how well it is known to the public.
Clarity: We can assume that it is easy for non-scientists to understand what the indicator is conveying and to interpret its variations. The indicator does not have uncertainties reported.
Reaction: Due to the well-established nature of this indicator within the scientific community and its clear meaning for non-scientists, it is expected that the response to information about it will be both appropriate and well-proportioned.

FullName: Mackerel average weight at age 3 in the stock
Description: Input for annual analytical stock assessment released by ICES in October. Data compile by WGWIDE. Weight-at-age (age 3) in the stock calculated as the average of the weight-at-age in three areas, corresponding to definition of the former spawning components, weighted by the relative mackerel biomass in each area (as estimated by the 2022 egg survey for the southern and western areas and the 2017 egg survey for the North Sea area). Mean weight-at-age in 2022 for the western area are estimated from Dutch, Irish and German commercial catch data, the biological sampling data taken during the egg surveys and during the Norwegian tagging survey. Only samples corresponding to mature fish, from areas and periods corresponding to spawning, as defined at the 2014 benchmark assessment (ICES, 2014) and laid out in the Stock Annex, were used to compute the mean weight-at-age in the western spawning area. For the North Sea area, mean weight-at-age in 2022 were calculated from samples of the commercial catches collected from Divisions 4.a and 4.b in the second quarter of 2022. Stock weights for the southern area, are based on samples from the Spanish catches and surveys in Divisions 8.c and 9a in the 2nd quarter of the year. The mean weights in the three area and in the stock in 2022 are shown in table 8.5.2.
Motivation: The indicator aims to estimate mackerel average weight-at-age, at stock level, during spawning time (spring). It measures average cumulative somatic growth of a mackerel year class during its juvenile stage, from hatch to age 3. At age 3, >90% of a cohort is mature. It contributes to IEA as it is a key variable for calculating stock biomass, in combination with numbers-at-age, and is also a key life history trait. The indictor is included in WGINOR Ecosystem Status Summary.
ConceptualUnderstanding: Weight-at-age is a key life history trait that reflects the somatic growth of a year class at a specific age. It provides insight into a fish's growth rate and how it allocates energy to growth over time. It impacts both survival and reproduction. It is also used to assess the health of a fish population, density dependnet growht, and can reflect the effects of environmental changes, such as shifts in ambient temperature or prey availability. It also serves as an indicator of evolutionary pressures on a stock.
ScientificConsensus: The indicator is widely accepted in the scientific community for use in stock assessment and for researching stock biology and ecology. It is used in many marine ecosystems and is also recommended by GOOS EOV as a key measurement for fish, as it is vital for calculating total fish biomass.
Precision: The indicator is a point estimate which is not reported with uncertainty estimates.
Bias: The level of bias is not estimated. Potential bias may come from high variability in the number and location of samples collected for each stock component from year to year. The bias can be expected to be low. We can trust absolute values unless specifically reported issues with sampling.
Updating: The indicator is updated annually. There is a year delay between measurement and reporting. The data are collected from March to June on the spawning grounds, and the index is reported in September the year after.  The indicator is calculated using all available commercial and survey samples from a predefined combinations of ICES divisions and months, which are considered representative of the spawning time and location for each of the three spawning components1. Stock level estimates are a weighted mean of the individual component estimates, with the weighting based on SSB estimated from the triannual egg survey.
SpaceTimeCoverage: The indicator has the spatial scale of the mackerel stock’s spawning area. It is calculated annually and integrated over the spawning season.
LevelOfIntegration: The level of integration is one year class of mackerel at the age when >90% of the year class is mature.
Cost: The indicator is produced by WGWIDE for annual stock assessment of mackerel. Sampling and calculation cost if coverage by stock assessment funding, from many nations, which can be considered secure funding in the long term.
History: The indicator has been estimated as an input series for stock assessment for decades. The current time series version begins in 1998, after the benchmark in March 2025.
Simplicity: The indicator is provided ready-to-use by another ICES working group. While the index is easy to compute, the challenging part is ensuring adequate spatial and temporal collection of samples.
Transparency: The calculations are described in ICES WGWIDE reports. The indicator values are available in TAF, but raw data used to calculate them are not.
DataType: The indicator is calculated using in situ data which are biological samples from commercial catches and research surveys. The raw data used to calculate the index are not publicly assessable. Contact person for the raw data is WGWIDE mackerel data coordinator: Afra Egan (Afra.Egan@marine.ie).
Sensitivity: Weight-at-age measures somatic growth of mackerel year-classes as they enter the spawning stock. The indicator is linearly related to changes in somatic growth between year classes. It is sensitive to changes in environmental conditions that influence growth, such as prey availability, temperature, and both intraspecific (within species) and interspecific (between species) competition for prey.
Specificity: Changes in weight-at-age is a response to changes in somatic growth of mackerel year classes as they enter the spawning stock. Weight-at-age is linearly related to changes in somatic growth (assuming non-biased sampling). Variations in weight-at-age are affected by several factors and cannot be interpreted unambiguously, since there are both accumulated responses (such as growth over time and competition) and more time limited responses (e.g. density dependent effects of year class strength based early in life).
LagAndLeadTime: The indicator response to changes in somatic growth between year-classes and on annual scale. The response is directly to the indicandum.
ManagementRelevance: The indicator is reported annually by ICES Working Group on Widely Distributed Stocks (WGWIDE) and is use in analytical stock assessment for mackerel.
ManagementUse: The indicator is primarily used for diagnostic purposes. It is used as input data in the mackerel stock assessment model.
ReferencePoint: There are no reference points for the indicator.The direction of change can be clearly interpreted. A higher weight-at-age 3 suggests that a year class experienced good somatic growth during its juvenile life history stage and that it is entering the spawning stock in better physiological state compared a year class with lower weight-at-age.
PriorKnowledge: The indicator is widely known to marine scientists and likely to most managers and stakeholders. It is difficult to assess how well it is known to the public.
Clarity: We can assume that it is easy for non-scientists to understand what the indicator is conveying and to interpret its variations. The indicator does not have uncertainties reported.
Reaction: Due to the well-established nature of this indicator within the scientific community and its clear meaning for non-scientists, it is expected that the response to information about it will be both appropriate and well-proportioned.

Description: NA
Motivation: Changes in the population size of Atlantic puffins breeding in the eastern Norwegian Sea indicates the status and trends for this endangered but still most numerous seabird species in mainland Norway. As the main driver of its population size is variation in breeding success that determines recruitment rates [1], it reflects temporal variation in the productivity of the pelagic food web on the Continental Shelf off Central Norway in spring and summer [2–5]. It complements parallel indicators for common guillemot and black-legged kittiwake, as these three species differ markedly in their foraging behaviour.
ConceptualUnderstanding: The indicator is an annual estimate of the total number of Atlantic puffins breeding along the eastern margin of the Norwegian Sea. The indicator responds to ecosystem changes mainly through food web effects on rates of adult survival and productivity, thus including indirect effects of fishing pressures and ocean climate variability. While breeding success reflects local conditions within reach of the colonies in spring and summer [2–5], survival of adult birds mainly reflects conditions in other sea areas in the non-breeding season [1,6,7], such as the Barents Sea in autumn and across the central Atlantic in winter [see https://seatrack.net/ for winter distributions]. Sub-lethal reduction of adult body condition in the wintering areas may also have carry-over effects on their reproductive performance in the following breeding season [1].
ScientificConsensus: The scientific consensus for this indicator is strong, and the data collection follows the international standards for such work [8]. The results are an integral part of the long ongoing reporting and assessments made by a variety of international expert groups on seabird ecology within ICES/OSPAR and CAFF. The stratified sampling approach developed at Røst [9] is implemented in several other seabird colonies in Norway as well as in the Antarctic (CCAMLR). The puffin usually feeds its chick bill-loads of many small fish taken by diving in the upper 70 m of the water column in pelagic waters on-shelf and beyond. It thereby supplements the two other pelagic seabird indicators, the common guillemot that feeds the chick single fish taken in the upper 180 m and the black-legged kittiwake that feeds its chicks regurgitates of small planktonic fish and crustaceans taken in the surface layer (< 1 m).
QuantitativeDimension: The indicator is measured quantitatively as the annual number of apparently occupied nest burrows (AOBs) in a variety of study plots distributed in four keysite colonies: Runde (62.4°N), Sklinna (65.2°N), Røst (67.5°N) and Anda (69.1°N). The scheme developed in Røst is estimated by a stratified sampling procedure [9].
Precision: The precision level is only estimated for one of the four monitoring colonies, but that site (Røst) used to have the largest puffin colony in the world and still holds close to 80% of the indicator population. The estimates there are derived from sampling 415 different 10 m² plots spread evenly across the entire colony surface to cover 2.5% of the area and producing a standard error of ± 5% of the final estimate [9]. The indicator also accounts for the colonies not included in the monitoring, by adjusting the most recent census of their population size with the trend at the closest keysite colony.
Bias: The level of bias is low. Although a small proportion of the population may refrain from breeding in a single year, most of those birds are still likely to visit their nest burrow frequently, thereby leaving behind the signs of occupancy that are captured by the monitoring.
Updating: The indicator is updated annually as an integrated part of the SEAPOP programme (www.seapop.no/en). The monitoring takes place in May (Røst) and June (elsewhere), and the data are entered into SEAPOP’s data base and reported to WGINOR in the same year. The time series for the indicator is only updated for and reported by WGINOR, but the keysite-specific results are reported together with parallel data on other performance indicators for the same populations in the annual reports published by SEAPOP at the programme website before the next breeding season.
SpaceTimeCoverage: The temporal scale is one year. The indicator covers four colonies, holding > 90% of the puffins breeding along the eastern coast of the Norwegian Sea. At the largest colony, Røst, only the island of Hernyken, one of the five biggest sub-colonies of puffins there, is monitored annually. The reported estimate is therefore upscaled to represent the whole archipelago, based on data from a total census of the Røst population made with the same sampling approach [10] The assumption is that population trends are uniform across the different islands, which is considered very likely given that the puffins always feed offshore, far outside the shallow Røst plateau. Predation from winged predators (eagles, gulls and raven) is also expected to be uniform on the five biggest islands, which hold 98.5% of the Røst population [10].
LevelOfIntegration: The primary level of integration is the regional breeding population size of Norway’s most numerous seabird species, the Atlantic puffin. Both the Faroes and Iceland have important populations of puffins, but these are not yet part of the WGINOR reporting. The indicator integrates bottom-up productivity of the coastal shelf ecosystem, from plankton via forage fish to seabird performance [e.g., 2–5,11]
Cost: The SEAPOP programme covers the field work with financial support from the Norwegian Environment Agency, the Norwegian Ministry of Energy, and Offshore Norge. The annual indicator update for WGINOR is done by participating scientists from NINA, mainly  by means of in-kind work and a small grant from the Norwegian Environment Agency.
History: The annual monitoring that forms the basis for the indicator estimates started in 1979 (Røst), 1980 (Runde), 1981 (Sklinna) and 2005 (Anda), respectively. The colony-specific time series are updated and reported annually as part of the SEAPOP programme.
Simplicity: The update procedure is relatively straightforward and does not involve heavy computation, but it requires some expertise on computing the proper input parameters from the SEAPOP monitoring data and how to update the population estimate based on the most recent colony-specific trends and census data.
Transparency: Because of limited funds, no detailed protocol for how to produce the indicator has yet been made, but the colony-specific input data are stored and managed by the SEAPOP programme (www.seapop.no/en) in accordance with the FAIR principles.
DataType: The data are based on in-situ data. All input data are contained in the national seabird data base administered and developed by the SEAPOP programme (www.seapop.no/en).
Sensitivity: As puffins start breeding when 5-7 years old [12], the indicator is a less responsive signal than indices of productivity (i.e., breeding success) or adult survival, which WGINOR may consider as candidate/supplementary indicators. The population size indicator results from the combined effects of variation in both adult survival and recruitment rates [1], making it a more ‘holistic’ indicator of the overall status of the indicator species. Moreover, it can also reflect very infrequent but drastic mass mortality events (“wrecks”) of adult birds, which almost exclusively occur outside the breeding season [e.g., 13,14]. The indicator responses are not necessarily linear as the productivity of key elements in the lower tropic chain up to the puffins’ prey – and the oceanographic processes affecting their availability – can be highly variable in time and space [e.g., 11].
Specificity: The indicator is general in nature as changes in its value cannot be attributed to a specific cause. It therefore does not allow for clear separation between the effects driven by ecosystem changes and those resulting from management actions.
LagAndLeadTime: In case of changes in conditions in the non-breeding areas at sea, the indicator can respond to ecosystem changes within one year. Otherwise, the response is usually caused by variation in food availability within the breeding season that affects productivity, which does not produce a signal in the indicator until the resulting cohort recruits to the breeding population at first breeding some 5-7 years later [12]. The indicator can be forecasted into the future if applying a more holistic modelling approach such as Integrated Population Models (IPM) that can combine effects of variation in external drivers of the key demographic parameters, i.e. adult survival rates and productivity [15,16].
ManagementRelevance: The population trends for the different keysite populations of puffins are reported individually and summarised in the annual open access reports published by the SEAPOP programme (www.seapop.no/en), which is run by the Norwegian Institute for Nature Research (NINA) and the Norwegian Polar Institute (NPI) in close cooperation with the Norwegian Environment Agency and several other authorities and private institutions. This type of monitoring data informs the main criterion for red listing of seabird species both nationally (by the Norwegian Biodiversity Information Centre) and internationally (by IUCN), namely the percentage change in population size over three generations. For the Atlantic puffin, this corresponds to 43 years (i.e., 1982 to date), given an estimated generation time of 14.2 years [17].
ManagementUse: The trends that form the basis for the indicator are reported both for surveillance and assessment of managements target, including integration in relevant international indicators of environmental status, such as the Ecological Quality Objectives (EcoQOs) developed by ICES/JWGBIRD for the OSPAR Commission and last reported in their Quality Status Report 2023 and Intermediate Assessment 2017 (https://oap.ospar.org/en/ospar-assessments/). Given the inherent delay in response to environmental change of population size indicators, only drastic changes that may signal massive wrecks of adults are likely to trigger more detailed studies that can uncover the underlying causes and identify relevant management actions.
ReferencePoint: A change in the indicator reflects a similarly directed change in environmental conditions experienced by the puffins. Up to now, reference levels for the size of seabird populations in Europe have often been set to equal the level registered when most of the time series were established (often early 1980s). This practice has however limited value for guiding management actions, besides allowing to set longer-term (3 generations) reference points for updating the red-list status of the species (see 5.1).
PriorKnowledge: Yes, especially the colony-specific changes integrated in the indicator. The puffin is an iconic and highly valued component of the marine environment along the Norwegian Sea coast. Its negative population trends and poor breeding performance in this region are often referred to by non-scientists, managers and many others as important indicators of the state of the marine environment.
Clarity: The state of the indicator species as such is easy to understand correctly but due to lack of quantitative knowledge on many of the potential driving forces and interactions involved, it is much more challenging to communicate the underlying mechanisms for the observed changes at different scales in time and space, and their associated uncertainties.
Reaction: Reactions to information about the indicator are expected to be adequate and proportional among the most informed scientists and managers but are expected to vary between other types of stakeholders and different groups of the broader audience. Most likely, they depend on varying perceptions about what are the main causes for negative change and what can be done to improve the situation. To bridge such information gaps and identify the best actions to improve ecosystem-based management, we need more quantitative knowledge on the underlying mechanisms and effects of what are expected to be the key drivers of change.

Description: NA
Motivation: Changes in the population size of black-legged kittiwake (hereafter kittiwake) indicates the status and trends for this endangered but still most numerous seabird species in mainland Norway. Its population trend reflects temporal variation in the productivity of the pelagic food web on the Continental Shelf off Central Norway in spring and summer [1-4]. It complements parallel indicators for Atlantic puffin and common guillemot, as these three seabird species differ markedly in their foraging behaviour.
ConceptualUnderstanding: The indicator is an annual estimate of the total number of kittiwakes breeding along the eastern margin of the Norwegian Sea. The indicator responds to ecosystem changes mainly through food web effects on rates of adult survival and productivity, thus including indirect effects of fishing pressures and ocean climate variability. While breeding success reflects local conditions within reach of the colonies in spring and summer [1–4], survival of adult birds also reflects conditions in other sea areas in the non-breeding season [5–7], such as the Barents Sea in autumn and in the western Atlantic in winter [see https://seatrack.net/ for winter distributions]. Sub-lethal reduction of adult body condition in the wintering areas may also have carry-over effects on their reproductive performance in the following breeding season [8–10].
ScientificConsensus: The scientific consensus for this indicator is strong, and the data collection follows the international standards for such work [11]. The results are an integral part of the long ongoing reporting and assessments made by a variety of international expert groups on seabird ecology within ICES/OSPAR and CAFF [12–13]. The kittiwake feeds its chicks regurgitates of numerous small fish and zooplankton crustaceans taken by plunge-diving within < 1 m of the water surface in pelagic waters on-shelf and beyond. It thereby supplements the two other pelagic seabird indicators that dive much deeper for their prey, the Atlantic puffin that feeds its chick bill-loads of small fish taken at depths < 70 m and the common guillemot that feeds the chick single fish taken at depths < 180 m.
QuantitativeDimension: The indicator is measured quantitatively as the number of apparently occupied nests (AONs) counted annually, either in designated study plots (the two largest colonies) or as total counts (the other colonies), in four keysite areas: Runde and Ålesund (62.4°N & 62.5°N), Sklinna and Sør-Gjeslingan (65.2°N & 64.7°N), Røst (67.5°N) and Anda (69.1°N).
Precision: The precision level for the sub-sampling monitoring schemes in the two largest colonies cannot not be estimated directly, and these colonies are now extinct (Vedøy in Røst) or close to extinction (Runde south of Ålesund). The monitoring plots were selected based on qualitative assessments of their representativeness and therefore expected to be high (likely less than ± 10%). Occasional whole-colony counts also allowed upscaling to total population numbers at these sites. The indicator also accounts for a decreasing number of smaller colonies not included in the monitoring, by adjusting their most recent census of their population size with the trend at the closest keysite colony.
Bias: The level of bias is low. Although a small proportion of the population may refrain from breeding in a single year, most of those birds are still expected to visit the colony frequently to defend their established nest site, thereby indicating occupancy that are captured by the monitoring.
Updating: The indicator is updated annually as an integrated part of the SEAPOP programme (www.seapop.no/en). The monitoring takes place in June, and the data are entered into SEAPOP’s data base and reported to WGINOR in the same year. The time series for the indicator is only updated for and reported by WGINOR, but the keysite-specific results are reported together with parallel data on other performance indicators for the same populations in the annual reports published by SEAPOP at the programme website before the next breeding season.
SpaceTimeCoverage: The temporal scale is one year. The indicator is based on population trends in four colony areas, traditionally known to hold a large part of the kittiwake population breeding along the eastern coast of the Norwegian Sea [4,15].
LevelOfIntegration: The primary level of integration is the regional breeding population size of Norway’s most numerous gull species, the black-legged kittiwake. Both the Faroes and Iceland have important kittiwake populations, but these are not yet part of the WGINOR reporting. The indicator integrates bottom-up productivity of the coastal shelf ecosystem, from plankton and forage fish to seabird performance [2,15]
Cost: The SEAPOP programme covers the field work with financial support from the Norwegian Environment Agency, the Norwegian Ministry of Energy, and Offshore Norge. The annual indicator update for WGINOR is done by participating scientists from NINA, mainly  by means of in-kind work and a small grant from the Norwegian Environment Agency.
History: The annual monitoring that forms the basis for the indicator estimates started in 1979 (Røst), 1980 (Runde), 1981 (Sklinna) and 2005 (Anda), respectively. The colony-specific time series are updated and reported annually as part of the SEAPOP programme.
Simplicity: The update procedure is relatively straightforward and does not involve heavy computation, but it requires some expertise on computing the proper input parameters from the SEAPOP monitoring data and how to update the population estimate based on the most recent colony-specific trends and census data.
Transparency: Because of limited funds, no detailed protocol for how to produce the indicator has yet been made, but the colony-specific input data are stored and managed by the SEAPOP programme (www.seapop.no/en) in accordance with the FAIR principles.
DataType: The data are based on in-situ data. All input data are contained in the national seabird data base administered and developed by the SEAPOP programme (www.seapop.no/en).
Sensitivity: As kittiwakes usually start breeding when 4–5 years old [16], the indicator is a less responsive signal than indices of productivity (i.e., breeding success) or adult survival, which WGINOR may consider as candidate/supplementary indicators. The population size indicator results from the combined effects of variation in both adult survival and recruitment rates, making it a more ‘holistic’ indicator of the overall status of the indicator species. Moreover, it can also reflect very infrequent but drastic mass mortality events (“wrecks”) of adult birds, which – except for epidemic HPAI incidents [17] – almost exclusively occur outside the breeding season when most of these kittiwakes travel to winter off in the western part of the North Atlantic off Newfoundland and Labrador [5]. The indicator responses are not necessarily linear as the productivity of key elements in the lower tropic chain up to the kittiwake’s prey – and the oceanographic processes affecting their availability – can be highly variable in time and space [e.g., 15].
Specificity: The indicator is general in nature as changes in its value cannot be attributed to a specific cause. It therefore does not allow for clear separation between the effects driven by ecosystem changes and those resulting from management actions.
LagAndLeadTime: In case of changes in conditions in the non-breeding areas at sea, the indicator can respond to ecosystem changes within one year. Otherwise, the response is usually caused by variation in food availability within the breeding season that affects productivity, which does not produce a signal in the indicator until the resulting cohort recruits to the breeding population at first breeding some 4-5 years later [16]. The indicator can be forecasted into the future if applying a more holistic modelling approach such as Integrated Population Models (IPM) that can combine effects of variation in external drivers of the key demographic parameters, i.e. adult survival rates and productivity [18–19].
ManagementRelevance: The population trends for the different keysite populations of kittiwakes are reported individually and summarised in the annual open access reports published by the SEAPOP programme (www.seapop.no/en), which is run by the Norwegian Institute for Nature Research (NINA) and the Norwegian Polar Institute (NPI) in close cooperation with the Norwegian Environment Agency and several other authorities and private institutions. This type of monitoring data informs the main criterion for red listing of seabird species both nationally by the Norwegian Biodiversity Information Centre and internationally by IUCN, namely the percentage change in population size over three generations. For the Atlantic puffin, this corresponds to 30 years (i.e., 1995 to date), given an estimated generation time of 9.8  years [19].
ManagementUse: The trends that form the basis for the indicator are reported both for surveillance and assessment of managements target, including integration in relevant international indicators of environmental status, such as the Ecological Quality Objectives (EcoQOs) developed by ICES/JWGBIRD for the OSPAR Commission and last reported in their Quality Status Report 2023 and Intermediate Assessment 2017 (https://oap.ospar.org/en/ospar-assessments/). Given the inherent delay in response to environmental change of population size indicators, only drastic changes that may signal massive wrecks of adults are likely to trigger more detailed studies that can uncover the underlying causes and identify relevant management actions.
ReferencePoint: A change in the indicator reflects a similarly directed change in environmental conditions experienced by the kittiwakes. Up to now, reference levels for the size of seabird populations in Europe have often been set to equal the level registered when most of the time series were established (often early 1980s). This practice has however limited value for guiding management actions, besides allowing to set longer-term (3 generations) reference points for updating the red-list status of the species (see 5.1).
PriorKnowledge: Yes, especially the colony-specific changes integrated in the indicator. Kittiwakes are a character species of many magnificent bird cliffs in boreal and Arctic waters and a highly valued component of the marine environment along the Norwegian Sea coast. Their fast-decreasing populations and poor breeding success are increasingly more often referred to by non-scientists, managers and many others as important indicators of the state of the marine environment.
Clarity: The state of the indicator species as such is easy to understand correctly but due to lack of quantitative knowledge on many of the potential driving forces and interactions involved, it is much more challenging to communicate the underlying mechanisms for the observed changes at different scales in time and space, and their associated uncertainties.
Reaction: Reactions to information about the indicator are expected to be adequate and proportional among the most informed scientists and managers but are expected to vary between other types of stakeholders and different groups of the broader audience. Most likely, they depend on varying perceptions about what are the main causes for negative change and what can be done to improve the situation. To bridge such information gaps and identify the best actions to improve ecosystem-based management, we need more quantitative knowledge on the underlying mechanisms and effects of what are expected to be the key drivers of change.

Description: NA
Motivation: Changes in the population size of common guillemots breeding in the eastern Norwegian Sea indicate the status and trends for this seabird species, which is critically endangered in mainland Norway. Although changes can be driven by variation in both adult survival and recruitment rates determined by breeding success, the population stays within Norwegian waters throughout the year [see https://seatrack.net/ for winter distributions]. Its numerical trends will therefore largely reflect the temporal variation in environmental conditions and productivity of the pelagic food web on the Continental Shelf off Central Norway. It complements parallel indicators for Atlantic puffins and black-legged kittiwakes, as these three species differ markedly in their foraging behaviour.
ConceptualUnderstanding: The indicator is an annual estimate of the total number of common guillemots breeding along the eastern margin of the Norwegian Sea. The indicator responds to ecosystem changes mainly through food web effects on adult survival and productivity, thus including also indirect effects of fishing pressures and ocean climate variability. As the birds remain in Norwegian coastal waters throughout their annual cycle [see https://seatrack.net/ for winter distributions from Sklinna and Røst], these vital rates, and thus common guillemot population trends, will reflect local conditions on the Norwegian shelf, even if some adults take an autumn trip to the southern Barents Sea [1] or northern North Sea [2].
ScientificConsensus: The scientific consensus for this indicator is strong, and the data collection follows the international standards for such work [3]. The results are an integral part of the long ongoing reporting and assessments made by a variety of international expert groups on seabird ecology within ICES/OSPAR and CAFF. The common guillemot dive for fish prey in pelagic waters at depths down to 180 m and brings its chick only one fish per foraging trip. It thereby supplements the two other pelagic seabird indicators, the Atlantic puffin that feeds its chick multiple small fish taken at depths < 70 m and the black-legged kittiwake that feeds its chicks regurgitates of numerous small planktonic fish and crustaceans taken in the surface layer (< 1 m).
QuantitativeDimension: The indicator is measured quantitatively as the average number of individual adults attending breeding ledges or crevices in steep cliffs or among boulders in designated study plots at three keysite colonies: Runde (62.4°N), Sklinna (65.2°N) and Røst (67.5°N). The usually 5–10 counts are made on separate days spread across the incubation and early chick period of the guillemots.
Precision: The precision level is difficult to quantify exactly, but the study plots were originally selected based on qualitative assessments of their representativeness and therefore expected to be high (likely less than ± 20%). At Røst, the 395 fixed plots and sub-plots in the monitoring colony (Vedøy) were spread widely over three of its five large cliff faces and support approximately one half of the population on the island [4]. Occasional whole-colony estimates allow upscaling to total population numbers at all three sites. The indicator also accounts for a few other colonies not included in the annual monitoring (such as on several other islands in Røst), by adjusting the most recent census of their population size with the trend at the closest keysite colony.
Bias: Can the level of bias in the indicator be estimated? What is the level of bias? If quantitative estimates are not available, is it possible to qualify the bias as “high” or “low”? Do we trust more relative changes in the indicator than absolute values?
Updating: How frequently can the indicator be updated? How long is the delay between observation/measurement and reporting of the indicator?
SpaceTimeCoverage: What is the spatial and temporal extent of the indicator? Does it reflect a feature at the global scale, at the Norwegian Sea ecosystem scale, in a sub-region, along a transect, at a station, other? Is it integrated over multiple years, one year, a season, a cruise duration, or at a unique date & time?
LevelOfIntegration: The primary level of integration is the regional breeding population size of the largest species of auks in the Atlantic, the common guillemot. Both the Faroes and Iceland have important populations of common guillemots, but these are not yet part of the WGINOR reporting. The indicator integrates bottom-up productivity of the coastal shelf ecosystem, from plankton via forage fish to seabird performance [6-9] but can also reflect top-down effects of avian predators [10].
Cost: The SEAPOP programme covers the field work with financial support from the Norwegian Environment Agency, the Norwegian Ministry of Energy, and Offshore Norge. The annual indicator update for WGINOR is done by participating scientists from NINA, mainly  by means of in-kind work and a small grant from the Norwegian Environment Agency.
History: The annual monitoring that forms the basis for the indicator estimates started in 1960 (Røst), 1980 (Runde) and 1983 (Sklinna), respectively.  The colony-specific time series are updated and reported annually as part of the SEAPOP programme.
Simplicity: The update procedure is relatively straightforward and does not involve heavy computation, but it requires some expertise on computing the proper input parameters from the SEAPOP monitoring data and how to update the population estimate based on the most recent colony-specific trends and census data.
Transparency: Because of limited funds, no detailed protocol for how to produce the indicator has yet been made, but the colony-specific input data are stored and managed by the SEAPOP programme (www.seapop.no/en) in accordance with the FAIR principles.
DataType: The data are based on in-situ data. All input data are contained in the national seabird data base administered and developed by the SEAPOP programme (www.seapop.no/en).
Sensitivity: As common guillemots usually start breeding when 5-7  years old [11], the indicator is a less responsive signal than indices of productivity (i.e., breeding success) or adult survival, which WGINOR may consider as candidate/supplementary indicators. As the population size indicator results from the combined effects of variation in both adult survival and recruitment rates, it is a more ‘holistic’ indicator of the overall status of the indicator species. It can however also reflect drastic mass mortality events (“wrecks”) of adult birds, which almost exclusively occur outside the breeding season and often hit large numbers of common guillemots [12-15]. The indicator responses are not necessarily linear as the productivity of key elements in the lower tropic chain up to the guillemots’ main prey – and the oceanographic processes affecting their availability – can be highly variable in time and space [e.g., 9].
Specificity: The indicator is general in nature as changes in its value cannot be attributed to a specific cause. It therefore does not allow for clear separation between the effects driven by ecosystem changes and those resulting from management actions.
LagAndLeadTime: In case of changes in conditions in the non-breeding areas at sea, the indicator can respond to ecosystem changes within one year. Otherwise, the response is usually caused by variation in food availability within the breeding season that affects productivity, which does not produce a signal in the indicator until the resulting cohort recruits to the breeding population at first breeding some 5-7 years later [11]. The indicator can be forecasted into the future if applying a more holistic modelling approach such as Integrated Population Models (IPM) that can combine effects of variation in external drivers of the key demographic parameters, i.e. adult survival rates and productivity [16].
ManagementRelevance: The population trends for the different keysite populations of common guillemots are reported individually and summarised in the annual open access reports published by the SEAPOP programme (www.seapop.no/en), which is run by the Norwegian Institute for Nature Research (NINA) and the Norwegian Polar Institute (NPI) in close cooperation with the Norwegian Environment Agency and several other authorities and private institutions. This type of monitoring data informs the main criterion for red listing of seabird species both nationally (by the Norwegian Biodiversity Information Centre) and internationally (by IUCN), namely the percentage change in population size over three generations. For the common guillemot, this corresponds to 44 years (i.e., 1981 to date), given an estimated generation time of 14.8 years [17].
ManagementUse: The trends that form the basis for the indicator are reported both for surveillance and assessment of managements target, including integration in relevant international indicators of environmental status, such as the Ecological Quality Objectives (EcoQOs) developed by ICES/JWGBIRD for the OSPAR Commission and last reported in their Quality Status Report 2023 and Intermediate Assessment 2017 (https://oap.ospar.org/en/ospar-assessments/). Given the inherent delay in response to environmental change of population size indicators, only drastic changes that may signal massive wrecks of adults are likely to trigger more detailed studies that can uncover the underlying causes and identify relevant management actions.
ReferencePoint: A change in the indicator reflects a similarly directed change in environmental conditions experienced by the common guillemots. Up to now, reference levels for the size of seabird populations in Europe have often been set to equal the level registered when most of the time series were established (often early 1980s). This practice has however limited value for guiding management actions, besides allowing to set longer-term (3 generations) reference points for updating the red-list status of the species (see 5.1).
PriorKnowledge: Yes, especially the colony-specific changes integrated in the indicator. The common guillemot is an archetypical inhabitant of some of the world’s most spectacular bird cliffs and a highly valued component of the marine environment along the Norwegian coast. Its negative population trends and poor breeding performance in this region are increasingly more often referred to by non-scientists, managers and many others as important indicators of the state of the marine environment. This is also triggered by a parallel increase in massive wreck events of the species outside the breeding season, especially in the North Sea and Skagerrak [18].
Clarity: The state of the indicator species as such is easy to understand correctly but due to lack of quantitative knowledge on many of the potential driving forces and interactions involved, it is much more challenging to communicate the underlying mechanisms for the observed changes at different scales in time and space, and their associated uncertainties.
Reaction: Reactions to information about the indicator are expected to be adequate and proportional among the most informed scientists and managers but are expected to vary between other types of stakeholders and different groups of the broader audience. Most likely, they depend on varying perceptions about what are the main causes for negative change and what can be done to improve the situation. To bridge such information gaps and identify the best actions to improve ecosystem-based management, we need more quantitative knowledge on the underlying mechanisms and effects of what are expected to be the key drivers of change.

Description: NA
Motivation: The component reported is the annual catch of common minke whale. It corresponds to the harvesting pressure (species extraction) and target a key species.  Catch trends provides insight into the harvesting pressure and how it has change through time.
ConceptualUnderstanding: The indicator measures the annual number of common minke whales caught by NAMMCO member countries (Faroes, Iceland, Norway) and reported officially.
ScientificConsensus: This indicator is widely accepted in marine mammal management. As for most of the indicators describing human activities, the minke whale catch is not recognized as an Essential Ocean Variable.
QuantitativeDimension: Quantitative, count of Minke whale individuals catch per year.
Precision: The level of precision is rather high. All legal catches are reported in the NAMMCO data base.
Bias: There is no estimation of bias available. If there is a bias, it would be toward an under-estimation of the catches if there are illegal or unreported catches (e.g. struck and lost). Legal catches are expected to be well reported.
Updating: The indicator is updated annually shortly after the hunting season that usually occurs April to august.
SpaceTimeCoverage: Minke whale catches are registered for the Northeast Atlantic, east of Greenland. The area is much greater than the Norwegian Sea ecoregion.
LevelOfIntegration: Provides species-level pressure insights. It integrates across fleets and jurisdictions during the whole hunting season.
Cost: The cost is very low, WGINOR requires minimal processing effort—simple extraction of data from the online database (they are already aggregated by region and by country).
History: The time series starts in 1993
Simplicity: The indicator is provided by NAMMCO, and no further processing of the data is required.
Transparency: NAMMCO catch database is public and searchable [I need to identify were is the documentation about the methodology (or ask NAMMCO)]
DataType: In situ catch records, available on the open and online NAMMCO catch database
Sensitivity: Increase in the real catch of Minke whale will linearly increase the official record of minke whale catch.
Specificity: It is unambiguous, only an increase in the catch would lead to an increase in the Minke whale catch indicator.
LagAndLeadTime: There is no lag between the indicator and the indicandum
ManagementRelevance: Highly relevant and formally used by NAMMCO and national agencies
ManagementUse: For NAMMCO it is a decision support
ReferencePoint: The directionality is clear: higher catch = greater harvesting pressure. The upper limit to catch is the annual quota.
PriorKnowledge: Known among marine mammal scientists, managers.
Clarity: The indicator is easily interpretable: a simple count of whales caught annually. Clarifying context (e.g. quota) might be important.
Reaction: Whales are iconic species and the response to the public can be unproportional and might even be unrelated for the indicator itself

Description: NA
Motivation: Fishing is the main pressure in the Norwegian Sea ecoregion. Fishing is a profitable activity and the price that fishermen are paid for the fish they catch (i.e. First-hand price) is a driver of the activity. The time series of First-hand price, allow to track changes in the socio-economic performance of the main pelagic fisheries. It can reflect how the market responds to fish population fluctuations (the price increase when the resource becomes scare) or reflect changes in the demand for fish. Support a more integrated perspective on the ecosystem beyond natural indicators.
ConceptualUnderstanding: First-hand-price is the price per kilogram paid directly to fishermen at auction for the landings of herring, mackerel and blue whiting (Sogn-Grundvåg et al. 2019). All sales for those three species are done by the Norwegian sale Organization for Pelagic fish (Norges sildesalgslag). There is one annual time-series per species.
ScientificConsensus: This indicator seems to be widely used in fisheries economics but not in natural science. This indicator is not part of GOOS.
QuantitativeDimension: Quantitative, recorded in NOK per kg, based on total sales value divided by total landed weight. One time series per stock.
Precision: Precision is expected to be high at the annual scale. The auctions are electronic, and all sales are recorded automatically.
Bias: No estimation of the bias.
Updating: The indicator time series can be updated annually
SpaceTimeCoverage: The first-hand price indicator corresponds to Norwegian pelagic fleet landings, covering all Norwegian EEZ and international water. The indicator integrates the fish sales annually.
LevelOfIntegration: The indicator focusses on three species catches: herring, mackerel and blue whiting
Cost: The indicator is produced by Fiskeridirektoratet with minimal additional effort required for WGINOR. For now, the data is only accessible in a pdf report which limits the automisation of the indicator update. The indicator is then corrected by multiplying it by the annual CPI (Consumer Price Index), available from SSB.no.
History: The time series is available from 2004 and the methodology has been consistent since then. Some minor changes may have occurred in the auction process (e.g. change in the sale species size categories), but given the degree of integration (species, annual) this is assumed to have no impact.
Simplicity: The process is very easy. It consists of extracting the values from a pdf and multiply them by the consumer CPI.
Transparency: The process is not available in TAF, there is not much transparency about how Fiskeridirektoratet compute the indicator.
DataType: In situ, the source is a new report produced by Fiskeridirektoratet each year: Economic and biological key figures 2024
Sensitivity: The indicator is an annual average of the FH-price which fluctuate through the year. If the variation throughout the year changes, the indicator would not allow to detect it.
Specificity: Yes, a change in the indicator lead directly to a change in the indicandum. However, what causes changes in the indicator can be more than just a change in the demand or the scarcity of catches. The sale organisation (Norges Sildesalgslag) can set minimum price (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.marpol.2018.06.011), exchange rate fluctuations (for exports), or other market interventions.
LagAndLeadTime: The response is immediate. Whether the indicator can be used to anticipate future change is uncertain (outside the expertise of the group for now)
ManagementRelevance: The indicator is reported by the Norwegian Directorate of Fisheries.
ManagementUse: For us it is a surveillance indicator.
ReferencePoint: The direction of change is clearly interpretable.
PriorKnowledge: The indicator is already known by fisheries sector actors, from researcher in socio-economic sciences. Not sure about the rest.
Clarity: Easy to explain: "average value per kg paid to fishers."

FullName: Total Catch all species combined in ICES Iia
Description: NA
Motivation: The Ryther index is the annual total catch per km2
ConceptualUnderstanding: The Ryther index is linked to species extraction from fisheries (pressure). The index is a direct measurement of the pressure and considers all exploited (+bycatch) species together and all fishing nations operating in the region.
ScientificConsensus: There is a general consensus on the indicator given that the reported catches represent accurately the actual catches, which is a reasonable assumption in the Norwegian Sea. The indicator has been used, for research purpose, in the global ocean.
Precision: It is not possible to estimate the uncertainty because catch statistics are provided as point estimates only. The uncertainty is assumed to be low to moderate, considering misreporting and other related issues.
Bias: There is not estimation of the bias. If there is a bias it is likely negative (underreporting) and it is assumed to be low to moderate because catch statistics in the area are considered reliable.
Updating: The indicator can be updated yearly from ICES Catch statistics. The is a three year delay between catches and availability of reporting.
SpaceTimeCoverage: The spatial extent of the indicator is the FAO27.2.a. which doesn’t fully align with the ICES Norwegian sea ecoregion. The indicator is integrated over a year and for the entire region. The time series is available from 2006.
LevelOfIntegration: Fish & invertebrate community both pelagic and demersal. It represents the pression from the whole fisheries sector.
Cost: The Nominal catch data used to compute the Ryther index are publicly available from ICES and in a standardised format. Some minor data wrangling is required to compute the index.
History: The ICES catch data dates back to 1903. Today’s catch data have been harmonised since 2006. It might be possible to reconstruct the indicator further back in time, but this would require additional work.
Simplicity: The calculation of the index is very simple.
Transparency: The principles of the Ryther calculation are given in Link et al 2019 and it is a simple summing of the catch (t) divided by the area (km2). The Ryther index is available in the WGINOR data table and does not fully adhere to FAIR principles.
DataType: NA
Sensitivity: The indicator responds directly and in a linear way to changes in fisheries pressure (catch)
Specificity: The indicator is specific. It could also vary if the quality of catch reporting changes.
LagAndLeadTime: No lag no lead between pressure (catch) and indicator value (Ryther index)
ManagementRelevance: The indicator is not currently used or requested by management. It is well suited to be integrated in the current fisheries management framework.
ManagementUse: Today, this is a surveillance indicator. It could be upgraded to diagnostic or decision support
ReferencePoint: The direction of change is directly interpretable: higher values indicate more catch. There are theoretical limit reference points, but these have neither been estimated nor agreed upon for the Norwegian Sea.
PriorKnowledge: The indicator is only known by few fisheries’ scientist involved in Ecosystem based Fisheries management.
Clarity: The interpretation is straightforward; communication is expected to be easy.
Reaction: Without reference point the reaction is expected to be proportional. If a reference point is defined, then the reaction is expected to be stronger when the index crosses the reference point.
